title: Tyler, Nabeel, and Jackson on French thinkers - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: In a podcast conversation excerpt, Tyler Cowen and Nabeel Qureshi assess major French theorists — dismissing Jacques Derrida as having 'no there there,' rating Baudrillard and Foucault more favorably despite flaws, remaining genuinely undecided on Lacan, and noting that Nabeel now has GPT explain Foucault to him rather than reading the primary texts himself.

insights.claim: Tyler Cowen concludes, after putting in significant effort to read him, that Jacques Derrida has 'no there there' and can be written off, while Nabeel Qureshi has never read Derrida and remains unconvinced there is a reason to.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A concrete, negative verdict on a canonical postmodern thinker from a widely-read generalist economist, useful signal for how much intellectual capital to allocate to Derrida.
insights.quote: I put in a fair amount of effort and did conclude, rightly or wrongly, that there’s no there there.

insights.claim: Tyler Cowen singles out Jacques Lacan as his one remaining 'marginal case' of possible substance in French theory — despite repeated attempts he has not concluded Lacan is empty, and plans to try a new Lacan book coming out later in the summer.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Flags which figure in the French-theory canon still merits a second look by Cowen's own standard, a useful marker for tracking his eventual public verdict.
insights.quote: Lacan is my marginal case of “no there there.”

insights.claim: Nabeel Qureshi argues modern French and postmodern philosophy generally puts too much of a premium on sounding cool rather than substance, though he thinks grasping the core ideas repays some effort short of making it one's life's reading.
insights.speaker: Nabeel Qureshi
insights.so_what: A calibrated middle position on French theory — worth some study time but not deep specialization — useful for readers deciding how to allocate intellectual effort.
insights.quote: I think modern French thinkers put too much of a premium on sounding cool, or postmodern philosophy generally.

insights.claim: Tyler Cowen rates Jean Baudrillard as 'quite good' and Michel Foucault as 'extremely interesting,' distinguishing them favorably from Derrida and signaling he is not broadly dismissive of French theory as a whole.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Clarifies that Cowen's skepticism is targeted at specific thinkers (Derrida) rather than the French tradition broadly, useful for interpreting his other commentary on continental philosophy.
insights.quote: Baudrillard is quite good and Foucault is extremely interesting.

themes.theme: AI as an intermediary for dense theory

insights.claim: Nabeel Qureshi now runs Foucault's writing through GPT and has the model explain it to him rather than reading the primary texts directly, treating an LLM as an acceptable substitute interpretive layer for dense academic philosophy.
insights.speaker: Nabeel Qureshi
insights.so_what: An early revealed-preference data point on AI-native readers substituting LLM summarization for primary-source engagement with dense academic material — relevant to tracking usage patterns for AI in humanities education and publishing.
insights.quote: I just put Foucault through GPT and I just have GPT explain it to me, and that’s going to be good enough for now.

insights.claim: Tyler Cowen argues Michel Foucault's historical claims are wrong 'in a quite mundane way' and that his core ideas about power structures are 'almost too simple,' and observes that the fact 'the current right' has latched onto Foucault is itself a sign of that simplicity.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Flags a notable ideological cross-pollination — Foucauldian power-structure analysis being adopted by right-leaning thinkers — a data point for tracking intellectual and political realignment narratives.
insights.quote: the current right has so latched on to Foucault is a sign that it’s simple.
